title: Discovery of Hydrogen
description: Cavendish isolated 'inflammable air' and showed it was a distinct substance, later proving that burning it produces water — a key step toward understanding chemical composition.

title: Discovery of Oxygen
description: Priestley and Scheele independently isolated oxygen. Its role in combustion overturned the phlogiston theory and reframed chemical reactions as combinations of real substances.

title: Conservation of Mass
description: Through meticulous weighing, Lavoisier established that mass is neither created nor destroyed in chemical reactions — the foundation of stoichiometry and of chemistry as a quantitative science.

title: Stoichiometry
description: Richter coined the term 'stoichiometry' and showed that substances combine in fixed mass ratios, allowing chemists to calculate the quantities involved in reactions.

title: Law of Combining Volumes
description: Gay-Lussac found that gases react in simple whole-number volume ratios, providing crucial evidence that would later support Avogadro's hypothesis and molecular formulas.

title: Synthesis of Urea
description: Wöhler synthesized urea from inorganic starting materials, showing that compounds made by living things obey the same chemistry as everything else.

title: Modern Titration & the Burette
description: Mohr's improved burette and textbook on volumetric analysis standardized titration, turning it into the precise analytical technique students still use today.

title: Ionic Dissociation
description: Arrhenius proposed that salts split into charged ions in water — initially met with skepticism, this idea underlies net ionic equations, conductivity, and solution chemistry.

title: Law of Definite Proportions
description: Proust showed that a given compound always contains the same elements in the same proportions by mass, a regularity that demanded an atomic explanation.

title: Law of Multiple Proportions
description: Dalton observed that when two elements form multiple compounds, the mass ratios are small whole numbers — striking evidence that matter combines in discrete units.

title: Atomic Theory
description: Dalton proposed that elements consist of indivisible atoms of characteristic mass, explaining the laws of chemical combination and launching modern chemistry.

title: Avogadro's Hypothesis
description: Avogadro proposed that equal volumes of gases contain equal numbers of particles — the insight behind the mole concept, ignored for nearly fifty years.

title: Table of Atomic Weights
description: Berzelius measured remarkably accurate atomic weights for dozens of elements and introduced the letter-based chemical symbols still in use today.

title: Consistent Atomic Weights
description: At the Karlsruhe Congress, Cannizzaro used Avogadro's hypothesis to put atomic weights on a consistent footing — the cleanup that made the periodic table possible.

title: The Electron
description: Thomson's cathode ray experiments revealed a particle far lighter than any atom, proving atoms are divisible and opening the door to atomic structure.

title: Charge of the Electron
description: Millikan's oil-drop experiment measured the fundamental unit of electric charge, pinning down the electron's mass and confirming charge is quantized.

title: The Nucleus
description: The gold-foil experiment showed that an atom's positive charge and nearly all its mass sit in a tiny central nucleus, replacing the 'plum pudding' model.

title: Mass Spectrometry & Isotopes
description: Aston's mass spectrograph separated atoms by mass and revealed that elements are mixtures of isotopes — the technique behind the mass spectra AP students interpret.

title: The Neutron
description: Chadwick identified the neutron, completing the modern picture of the atom and explaining isotopes as atoms differing only in neutron count.

title: Hydrogen Spectral Lines
description: Balmer found a simple numerical formula for hydrogen's visible spectral lines — a pattern with no explanation until Bohr's model arrived 28 years later.

title: Quantization of Energy
description: To explain blackbody radiation, Planck proposed that energy is exchanged in discrete packets (quanta) — the reluctant first step of the quantum revolution.

title: Photoelectric Effect
description: Einstein explained the photoelectric effect by treating light as particles of energy E = hν, the relationship students use to connect light and electron energies.

title: Bohr Model
description: Bohr quantized the electron's orbits in hydrogen, explaining its spectral lines exactly and introducing energy levels — the mental model that still anchors introductory chemistry.

title: Matter Waves
description: De Broglie proposed that electrons behave as waves as well as particles, the conceptual leap from Bohr's orbits to quantum-mechanical orbitals.

title: Exclusion Principle
description: Pauli's rule that no two electrons can share the same quantum state explains why electrons fill shells — and therefore why the periodic table has its shape.

title: Quantum Mechanics
description: Schrödinger's wave equation (and Heisenberg's equivalent matrix mechanics) replaced orbits with orbitals — probability clouds that remain our deepest description of electrons in atoms.

title: Uncertainty Principle
description: Heisenberg showed that an electron's position and momentum cannot both be known precisely, explaining why orbitals describe probabilities rather than paths.

title: Photoelectron Spectroscopy
description: Siegbahn's high-resolution photoelectron spectroscopy measured the binding energies of electrons in atoms — the PES spectra that AP Chemistry students learn to read.

title: Döbereiner's Triads
description: Döbereiner noticed trios of chemically similar elements with evenly spaced atomic weights — the first hint of periodicity among the elements.

title: Law of Octaves
description: Newlands arranged elements by atomic weight and saw properties repeat every eighth element — ridiculed at the time, vindicated by Mendeleev four years later.

title: Periodic Table
description: Mendeleev organized the elements by weight and property, boldly leaving gaps and predicting the elements that would fill them — chemistry's most iconic organizing framework.

title: Discovery of the Noble Gases
description: Ramsay and Rayleigh discovered argon, then the rest of the noble gases — an entire unreactive family the periodic table had not predicted, which it gracefully absorbed.

title: Atomic Number
description: Moseley's X-ray spectra showed each element has a unique nuclear charge, reordering the periodic table by atomic number rather than weight and fixing its anomalies.

title: Concept of Valence
description: Frankland recognized that each element has a fixed combining capacity, the concept of valence that made structural formulas conceivable.

title: Structural Theory of Carbon
description: Kekulé and Couper proposed that tetravalent carbon atoms link into chains, founding structural chemistry — the idea that molecules have architecture.

title: Tetrahedral Carbon
description: Van 't Hoff and Le Bel independently argued that carbon's four bonds point to the corners of a tetrahedron — molecules became three-dimensional objects.

title: X-ray Crystallography
description: The Braggs used X-ray diffraction to determine the arrangement of ions in crystals like NaCl, revealing that ionic solids are lattices, not molecules.

title: Ionic Bonding Theory
description: Kossel explained ionic bonding as electron transfer that leaves both ions with noble-gas configurations, published the same year as Lewis's shared-pair theory.

title: Lewis Structures
description: Lewis proposed that covalent bonds are shared electron pairs and drew the dot structures that bear his name — still the first tool chemists reach for when sketching a molecule.

title: Quantum Theory of the Covalent Bond
description: Heitler and London used the new quantum mechanics to calculate the H₂ bond, showing that Lewis's shared pairs emerge naturally from wave mechanics.

title: Hybridization & Valence Bond Theory
description: Pauling's hybrid orbitals (sp³, sp², sp) reconciled quantum mechanics with the observed geometries of molecules like methane.

title: Molecular Orbital Theory
description: Mulliken and Hund described bonding with orbitals spread over whole molecules, explaining phenomena — like O₂'s paramagnetism — that Lewis structures cannot.

title: Electronegativity Scale
description: Pauling quantified how strongly atoms pull on shared electrons, giving chemists the scale used to predict bond polarity across the periodic table.

title: VSEPR Theory
description: Gillespie and Nyholm systematized the idea that electron pairs repel each other into predictable geometries — the VSEPR rules students use to predict molecular shapes.

title: Boyle's Law
description: Boyle showed that a gas's pressure and volume are inversely related — one of the first quantitative laws in science, over a century before modern chemistry.

title: Charles's Law
description: Charles found (and Gay-Lussac later published) that gas volume grows linearly with temperature, pointing toward an absolute zero of temperature.

title: Law of Partial Pressures
description: Dalton showed that each gas in a mixture exerts its own independent pressure — evidence that gases are made of freely moving, non-interacting particles.

title: Ideal Gas Law
description: Clapeyron combined Boyle's, Charles's, and Avogadro's laws into a single equation of state, PV = nRT — the workhorse relation of gas chemistry.

title: Graham's Law of Effusion
description: Graham found that lighter gases escape through small openings faster than heavier ones, in inverse proportion to the square root of their molar mass.

title: Kinetic Molecular Theory
description: Maxwell and Boltzmann derived gas behavior from molecules in random motion, including the distribution of molecular speeds that explains temperature itself.

title: Real Gases & Intermolecular Forces
description: Van der Waals corrected the ideal gas law for molecular size and attraction, providing the first quantitative evidence for forces between molecules.

title: The Hydrogen Bond
description: Latimer and Rodebush described the hydrogen bond to explain water's unusual properties — the interaction that shapes everything from boiling points to DNA.

title: London Dispersion Forces
description: London used quantum mechanics to explain why even nonpolar atoms attract each other — instantaneous dipoles that give every substance some stickiness.

title: Henry's Law
description: Henry found that a gas's solubility in a liquid is proportional to its pressure — why soda fizzes when opened and why divers worry about depth.

title: Beer–Lambert Law
description: Beer extended Lambert's work on light absorption to solutions: absorbance is proportional to concentration, the basis of spectrophotometry in every chemistry lab.

title: Raoult's Law
description: Raoult showed that a solvent's vapor pressure drops in proportion to dissolved solute, connecting solution composition to measurable physical properties.

title: Chromatography
description: Tsvet separated plant pigments by washing them through a column of powder, inventing chromatography — the separation technique underlying modern chemical analysis.

title: First Measurement of Reaction Rate
description: Wilhelmy tracked the inversion of sugar over time and wrote the first rate equation, founding chemical kinetics as a quantitative field.

title: Rate Laws & Reaction Order
description: In his 'Studies in Chemical Dynamics', van 't Hoff classified reactions by order and connected rate laws to concentration — the framework of AP kinetics.

title: Arrhenius Equation
description: Arrhenius explained why rates rise steeply with temperature: molecules must clear an activation energy barrier — the equation and the concept both bear his name.

title: Radioactive Decay & Half-Life
description: Rutherford and Soddy showed radioactive decay follows first-order kinetics with a characteristic half-life — nature's cleanest example of a rate law.

title: Catalysis
description: Berzelius named and unified scattered observations of substances that speed reactions without being consumed — catalysis, now central to industry and biology.

title: Enzyme Kinetics
description: Michaelis and Menten modeled how enzymes bind substrates and turn them over, showing biological catalysts obey the same kinetics as any mechanism.

title: Reaction Mechanisms
description: Lindemann explained 'unimolecular' reactions as multi-step sequences of collisions and breakdowns — the prototype for analyzing reactions as elementary steps.

title: Transition State Theory
description: Eyring and Polanyi pictured reactions passing through a fleeting high-energy transition state, giving activation energy a precise molecular meaning.

title: Latent & Specific Heat
description: Black distinguished heat from temperature and discovered that melting and boiling absorb heat without warming — the concepts behind every q = mcΔT problem.

title: Ice Calorimeter
description: Lavoisier and Laplace measured reaction heats by the ice they melted, inventing calorimetry — and showed respiration is a slow combustion.

title: Hess's Law
description: Hess found that reaction heats add like the reactions themselves: the enthalpy change is the same whatever the path — thermochemistry's bookkeeping principle.

title: Mechanical Equivalent of Heat
description: Joule's paddle-wheel experiments showed heat and work are interchangeable forms of energy, dissolving the idea of heat as a substance.

title: Conservation of Energy
description: Helmholtz gave the general statement of energy conservation — the first law of thermodynamics that governs every energy diagram in chemistry.

title: Bomb Calorimetry
description: Berthelot's sealed 'bomb' calorimeter made combustion heats measurable with precision, generating the tables of reaction enthalpies chemists rely on.

title: Enthalpy
description: Kamerlingh Onnes coined 'enthalpy' for the heat content H that chemists had been using implicitly, giving constant-pressure heat flow its modern name.

title: Reversible Reactions
description: Observing soda deposits by Egyptian salt lakes, Berthollet realized reactions can run backward when conditions change — the seed of chemical equilibrium.

title: Law of Mass Action
description: Guldberg and Waage expressed equilibrium as a ratio of concentrations — the equilibrium constant K that quantifies where every reaction comes to rest.

title: Le Châtelier's Principle
description: Le Châtelier stated that a system at equilibrium shifts to counteract disturbances — the intuition chemists use daily to reason about stress and response.

title: Temperature Dependence of K
description: Van 't Hoff related the equilibrium constant to temperature and reaction heat, explaining quantitatively why heating shifts equilibria.

title: Solubility Product
description: Nernst applied mass action to sparingly soluble salts, defining the solubility product Ksp that governs precipitation and dissolution.

title: Haber–Bosch Process
description: Haber used equilibrium principles — pressure, temperature, catalysis — to fix nitrogen from air into ammonia, arguably the most consequential applied equilibrium in history.

title: Hydrogen Theory of Acids
description: Davy proved muriatic (hydrochloric) acid contains no oxygen, overturning Lavoisier's oxygen theory of acidity and pointing to hydrogen as the key.

title: Acids as Replaceable Hydrogen
description: Liebig defined acids as compounds whose hydrogen can be replaced by metals — a working definition that guided chemistry for fifty years.

title: Phenolphthalein Indicator
description: Baeyer synthesized phenolphthalein, whose vivid pink endpoint made acid–base titrations dramatically easier to read.

title: Arrhenius Acid–Base Theory
description: Arrhenius defined acids as producers of H⁺ and bases as producers of OH⁻ in water — the first molecular theory of acidity.

title: Weak Acid Ionization
description: Ostwald connected a weak acid's degree of ionization to its dilution through an equilibrium constant — the Ka reasoning at the heart of AP acid–base chemistry.

title: Buffer Equation
description: Henderson derived, and Hasselbalch recast in logarithmic form, the equation relating a buffer's pH to its composition — indispensable from titration curves to blood chemistry.

title: The pH Scale
description: Working on beer quality at the Carlsberg Laboratory, Sørensen invented the pH scale — compressing ten orders of magnitude of acidity into a single convenient number.

title: Brønsted–Lowry Theory
description: Brønsted and Lowry independently redefined acids and bases as proton donors and acceptors, unifying acid–base chemistry around conjugate pairs.

title: Lewis Acids & Bases
description: Lewis generalized acidity further: acids accept electron pairs and bases donate them, capturing reactions that involve no protons at all.

title: The Voltaic Pile
description: Volta stacked zinc and copper discs into the first battery, producing steady electric current from chemistry and igniting the field of electrochemistry.

title: Electrolysis of Water
description: Weeks after Volta's announcement, Nicholson and Carlisle used the pile to split water into hydrogen and oxygen — electricity driving chemistry in reverse.

title: Elements Isolated by Electrolysis
description: Davy used massive batteries to rip potassium, sodium, and other new elements out of their compounds — electrochemistry as a tool of discovery.

title: Laws of Electrolysis
description: Faraday showed the amount of substance transformed at an electrode is proportional to the charge passed, linking moles to coulombs — and coined 'ion', 'anode', and 'cathode'.

title: Daniell Cell
description: Daniell's zinc–copper cell delivered stable voltage for hours, the practical galvanic cell whose diagram still opens every electrochemistry chapter.

title: Rechargeable Battery
description: Planté's lead–acid cell could be recharged by reversing the current — the same chemistry that still starts most cars today.

title: Entropy
description: Clausius defined entropy and stated the second law: the entropy of the universe tends to a maximum — the arrow that decides which changes are possible.

title: Gibbs Free Energy
description: In a dense, initially overlooked paper, Gibbs combined enthalpy and entropy into free energy, G = H − TS — the single quantity that predicts whether a reaction will go.

title: Statistical Entropy
description: Boltzmann explained entropy as counting molecular arrangements, S = k log W — connecting disorder at the molecular scale to the fate of reactions.

title: Nernst Equation
description: Nernst related a cell's voltage to the concentrations of its reactants, joining electrochemistry to thermodynamics in a single equation.

title: Third Law of Thermodynamics
description: Nernst's heat theorem — entropy approaches zero at absolute zero — made absolute entropies calculable, completing classical thermodynamics.
